evidence_gaps: No analytics, search logs, support tickets, or prior user research were provided.
The section mixes HHS pages with external CDC and FDA tools, so end-to-end completion varies by destination.
The page is a hub with limited explanatory copy; user frequency and impact had to be inferred from visible IA and linked destinations.
The provided business_context and constraints fields were not populated.

evidence.element: Learn More card: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
evidence.note: Promoted as a core transparency destination for searching contaminant levels in foods.

evidence.element: Search, advanced search, and downloadable Excel data
evidence.note: Tool supports direct lookup, filtering, browsing, and export.

task_longlist.rationale: This is one of the site’s most explicit value propositions, tied to food safety and supported by a working searchable tool with download capability.

task_longlist.task_statement: See ACIP members' conflicts of interest

evidence.element: Learn More card: CDC’s ACIP Conflicts of Interest
evidence.note: One of the three main transparency promises in the hero and card area.

evidence.element: Disclosure page overview and disclosure tool description
evidence.note: CDC page explains the disclosure history and that users can find prior disclosures by member.

task_longlist.rationale: Highly prominent and mission-aligned. Frequency is likely lower than food lookup, but the trust and accountability stakes are high.

task_longlist.task_statement: See which HHS contracts were terminated

evidence.element: Ending Wasteful Spending section
evidence.note: Explicitly invites users to view contracts terminated in full or part since January 21, 2025.

evidence.element: HHS Terminated Contracts PDF link
evidence.note: Dedicated page with direct link to the contract list and USAspending for additional detail.

task_longlist.rationale: This is a core promise of the section and is easy to find. Completion is slightly lower because the detailed list is a 54-page PDF.

task_longlist.task_statement: Learn what is in my food

evidence.element: Hero statement and Keeping Food Ingredients Safe section
evidence.note: Site explicitly frames transparency around contaminants and ingredients in food.

evidence.element: Press release on revising GRAS pathway
evidence.note: States the goal is to make sure Americans know what is in their food.

task_longlist.rationale: Likely a broad user need underlying several content areas, though the site partly informs rather than fully resolves the question end-to-end.

task_longlist.task_statement: Submit or check a FOIA request

evidence.element: Explore Radical Transparency link to FOIA
evidence.note: FOIA is surfaced as a major related transparency path.

evidence.element: New or Existing FOIA Requests section
evidence.note: FOIA page supports submitting a request and checking status.

task_longlist.rationale: For a general public audience, FOIA is a major transparency task with real stakes, but it is not presented as prominently as the three lead cards.

evidence.element: FOIA overview, exemptions, library, and reports
evidence.note: The page explains what FOIA is and links to exemptions, contacts, and reports.

task_longlist.rationale: Important precursor task for requesters, but more informational than transactional.

task_longlist.task_statement: Learn about food ingredient safety rules

evidence.element: Keeping Food Ingredients Safe section
evidence.note: Featured as a major section with explanation of GRAS oversight changes.

evidence.element: Rulemaking announcement
evidence.note: Explains the self-affirmed GRAS pathway and proposed transparency changes.

task_longlist.rationale: Relevant to food-conscious consumers and policy watchers, but the destination is primarily explanatory news content.

task_longlist.task_statement: Review open government data and plans

evidence.element: Explore link to Open Government at HHS
evidence.note: Open Government is surfaced as a related transparency resource.

evidence.element: Open Data and Plans and Policies sections
evidence.note: Provides links to HealthData.gov, Health IT Data, strategic plan, public access, and FOIA.

task_longlist.rationale: Useful for researchers and civically engaged users, but less likely to be a mass-audience need than the lead transparency tools.

evidence.element: Learn More card: Ending Anti-Semitism on College Campuses
evidence.note: Promoted on the landing page as a related transparency topic.

evidence.element: Press release content
evidence.note: Provides details on interagency review actions tied to campus anti-Semitism.

task_longlist.rationale: Important to a narrower audience and currently framed as an informational update rather than a full service journey.

evidence.element: Download data link
evidence.note: The contaminant tool explicitly offers Excel download.

task_longlist.rationale: Valuable for a smaller subset of advanced users, but not likely to be a dominant public task.

task_longlist.task_statement: Sign up for HHS email updates

evidence.element: HHS Email updates subscribe CTA
evidence.note: Persistent footer CTA offers email subscription.

task_longlist.rationale: Easy to complete but not central to why most visitors would come to this section.

evidence.element: Featured Topics: MAHA In Action and RealFood.gov
evidence.note: Landing page promotes related initiatives via outbound CTAs.

task_longlist.rationale: These are adjacent navigation paths rather than core transparency tasks for this section.

recommended_survey.instructions: Ask respondents to pick and rank the five tasks that matter most to them when visiting this section or a section like it. Randomize task order for each respondent. Use plain-language task labels only, with no HHS jargon or explanations unless a task is ambiguous. Include one optional free-text question asking what important task is missing.
task_list_for_voting: See ACIP members' conflicts of interest
See which HHS contracts were terminated
Learn what is in my food
Submit or check a FOIA request
Learn about food ingredient safety rules
Review open government data and plans
Sign up for HHS email updates
target_segments: General public visitors interested in food safety
People seeking government transparency or accountability information
FOIA requesters or prospective FOIA requesters

next_steps: Validate the top five tasks with a forced-choice Top Tasks survey using randomized order.
Check analytics and on-site search logs to confirm whether FOIA tasks and food-related tasks dominate real demand.
Test whether users can distinguish between 'food contaminants' and 'food ingredient safety rules' without confusion.
Reduce cross-program distraction on the landing page if MAHA and RealFood links pull attention away from core transparency tasks.
Improve task completion around terminated contracts by adding filterable HTML views or summaries instead of relying mainly on a long PDF.
Consider clearer separation between transparency tools, policy announcements, and related-news content.
summary: The strongest user tasks are checking contaminants in food, viewing ACIP conflicts of interest, seeing terminated HHS contracts, understanding what is in food, and submitting or checking a FOIA request. The section’s biggest strength is prominence: the first three tasks are clearly surfaced on the landing page. The biggest risk is mixed interaction models—some destinations are external tools, one key artifact is a long PDF, and some featured content is news rather than task completion. Next, validate whether visitors primarily want food transparency, accountability records, or FOIA access, and test whether adjacent promotional content distracts from those core tasks.
